retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Composition
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is an irreversible hydrocolloid material that can reproduce soft and hard tissue details when in the presence of water. Alginates are salts produced from the combination of alginic acid with either sodium, calcium, potassium, or magnesium. [3] Alginic acid is a polysaccharide extracted from brown algae, a member of the Phaeophyceae family found primarily in America. [3] Dental alginates are available as a powder designed to be mixed with water. The powder contains sodium or potassium alginates, filler particles, calcium sulfate (reactor), fluoride (accelerator), and sodium phosphate (retarder). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Equipment -- The most frequently utilized is the flour of pumice and calcium carbonate.
retrieved_docs.content: Feldspar: Used on teeth and restorations. Pumice: Used for polishing tooth enamel, gold foil, amalgam, and acrylic resins are siliceous materials. The disadvantage of pumice is its significant abrasive depth and average polishing capacity compared to other polishing agents. Calcium carbonate: Less abrasive than pumice, produces minimum scratches and a highly reflective surface. Perlite: A fluoride-containing abrasive agent. Aluminum silicate is an excellent stain remover with a great taste. It is easy to rinse off, has excellent polishing capacity, and releases fluoride. Xylitol-containing products: Help in saliva production and reduce dry mouth, thus helping to reduce decay, acid, and biofilm production in the mouth. They can be used in kids and are available in various sizes. Novamin containing products: Reduce the sensitivity and help in stain removal. Zirconium silicate: Used on discs and strips and prophylactic polishing pastes. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In contrast, the solid-state reaction yields a stoichiometric and well-defined crystalline structure of hydroxyapatite. However, this method requires high temperatures and extended heat treatment procedures. The raw materials used in the solid-state method should have a Ca/P ratio of 1.67 and must be ball-milled to ensure uniform particle size. [2] The solid-state reaction method solely depends on the solid diffusion of ions into the raw materials, thereby necessitating temperatures around 1250 ºC to initiate the reaction. [2] Moreover, prolonged heat treatment transforms singular crystalline particles into more blocky crystals, and the increase in crystalline size leads to a decrease in porosity, which is associated with the aging process. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Alginate is a hydrocolloid widely used as an impression material in dentistry. It is derived from alginic acid, present in brown algae. It is an irreversible and elastic impression material. [13] See Image. Alginate, Impression Making. 1. Composition: Sodium or potassium alginate (15%) - dissolves in water and forms a viscous sol Calcium sulfate (16%) - reactor Diatomaceous earth (60%) - filler Zinc oxide (4%) - filler Potassium titanium fluoride (3%) - accelerator Sodium phosphate (2%) - retarder Glycol (in dust-free alginates) Pigments and flavoring agents 2. Indications: Diagnostic casts for treatment planning Orthodontic study models Impressions to fabricate partial and complete dentures, and temporomandibular disorder appliances For duplicating models 3. Instruments and armamentarium: flexible rubber bowl, curved spatula, measuring scoop, perforated impression tray, alginate powder, and water.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Setting Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The setting mechanism of alginate is a chemical reaction between the salt sodium alginate and the reactor calcium sulfate. [3] This biphasic chemical reaction has a slowing phase and a setting phase. [3] When the powder is mixed with water, the calcium sulfate reactor initially combines with the sodium phosphate retarder, allowing for adequate working time. [3] After all the retarder is consumed, the remaining calcium sulfate reacts with the alginic salt to form insoluble calcium alginate in gel form. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Glass Ionomer Cement -- Function -- Mechanism of Setting
retrieved_docs.content: The setting of GIC is through an acid-base reaction involving the following stages: Dissolution and decomposition: after mixing the powder and liquid, the acid dissolves the surface of the glass particles to release SiO 4 4- , Ca 2+ , Na + , and F - ions. Initial setting: the Ca 2+ released in the aqueous medium reacts with polyacrylic acid to form a 3-dimensional cross-linked structure. At this time, the material has low strength, and it can be carved using sharp instruments. Final setting: the setting reaction continues during the next 24 hours. During this time, SiO 4 4- forms the silica gel. The slow-moving Al 3+ ions enter the aqueous medium and cross-link the polyacrylic acid chains, displacing the Ca 2+ ions. This increases the final strength of the set cement. Maturation: the aluminum and calcium cross-linked polyacrylate chains get hydrated over time by absorbing water from the aqueous medium. This process is known as maturation. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The wet method produces nonstoichiometric hydroxyapatite powder that may contain impurities such as hydrogen phosphate, carbonate, chloride, and sodium ions. These impurities can lead to the formation of calcium-deficient hydroxyapatite. [1] Previous studies have identified these impurities as uncontrollable variables that can significantly alter the crystallographic arrangement and chemical properties of hydroxyapatite, ultimately affecting its dissolution process.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Issues of Concern -- Setting Reaction and Interaction with Dentin
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine is a 2-part dental material with specific compositions for its powder and liquid components. The powder's primary component is tricalcium silicate. [2] Calcium carbonate and oxide act as fillers. [2] Zirconium oxide is added to the powder for X-ray visibility, and iron oxide is used for shade. [2] The liquid contains calcium chloride, acting as a setting accelerator and a water-reducing agent. [2] Calcium chloride accelerates the setting time of the material and prevents crack formation, a common issue in cement with high water content. The initial setting time for Biodentine is 9-12 minutes. [2] Saliva and blood contamination can delay setting. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite are influenced by factors such as phase composition, crystal size, and synthesis method. Pure hydroxyapatite typically has a bending strength of 38 to 250 MPa, compressive strength of 120 to 150 MPa, and tensile strength of 38 to 300 MPa. The Young modulus ranges from 35 to 120 GPa, depending on the presence of impurities. [19] Meanwhile, the Weibull modulus, ranging from 5 to 18, indicates hydroxyapatite is a brittle material. To enhance its mechanical properties, tough hydroxyapatite can be obtained by incorporating TCP, which increases its flexural strength. In contrast, the flexural strength reaches its minimum when hydroxyapatite contains calcium oxide CaO. [10] [19] Furthermore, the sintering temperature also significantly alters the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite. Higher sintering temperatures lead to increased density, compressive strength, grain size, and torsional strength. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Alginate Impressions -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Very few allergic reactions to alginates have been reported. Alginate impression material is contraindicated in patients with a severe allergy to crystalline silica, calcium sulfate, or potassium titanium fluoride.

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The restorations are processed either by soft machining of pre-sintered blanks or by hard machining of fully sintered blocks. The mechanical properties of 3Y-TZP are strongly affected by its grain size. The mechanical properties of zirconia have been reported to be higher than other ceramics for dental applications. Fracture resistance of 6–10 MPa/m1/2, a flexural strength of 900–1200 MPa, and a compression resistance of 2000 MPa have been reported. Zirconia restorations bear an average load of 755 N. Zirconia yields higher fracture loads than alumina or lithium disilicate. Zirconia ceramics yield superior wear behavior and lower antagonistic wear compared to porcelain.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Several methods exist for producing hydroxyapatite from synthetic or natural sources. Synthetic hydroxyapatite is derived from raw materials such as calcium carbonate, calcium hydroxide, calcium nitrate, diammonium hydrogen phosphate, and ammonium hydroxide. The fabrication process of hydroxyapatite involves both wet methods and solid-state reactions, followed by calcination or sintering. These methods utilize chemical reactions that adjust the content of CaO and TCP to achieve the stoichiometric conditions necessary for hydroxyapatite formation.

retrieved_docs.content: process without contributing therapeutic activity. Calcium carbonate is also present as an inactive ingredient in the oral rinse “sodium fluoride 0.5 MG/ML Mouthwash”, where it likely serves as a buffering agent. The supplement “Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron” is described as having a narrower relationship to calcium carbonate, indicating that calcium carbonate is a key, though not exclusive, component of its formulation. Finally, the material “perleť” (mother‑of‑pearl) is linked to calcium carbonate through a “has child” relationship, reflecting the natural derivation of nacre from calcium carbonate crystals. Collectively, these relationships illustrate calcium carbonate’s versatile role in human and veterinary medicines—as a therapeutic calcium source, a pH‑modulating agent, a filler or excipient, and even as a structural component of natural biomaterials—underscoring its importance across diverse therapeutic contexts and product types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals are a class of substances used in pharmaceutical preparation, and within this class calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are identified as specific members. Calcium compounds represent another category of calcium‑based substances, comprising calcium acetate, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate. Consequently, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are recognized both as bulk compounding chemicals and as calcium compounds, highlighting their dual role in formulation processes and calcium chemistry. This overlap underscores the importance of these agents in both compounding practices and calcium‑related therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2047
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Compounds represent a class of calcium‑containing chemical substances that encompass specific entities such as calcium acetate and kalcium‑glycerolfosfát. Both calcium acetate and kalcium‑glycerolfosfát are individual members of the broader category of Calcium Compounds, sharing the common characteristic of containing calcium and often being considered in medical or nutritional contexts where calcium supplementation or calcium‑based reactions are relevant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals refer to the inorganic substances used in large‑scale pharmaceutical preparation, and this category includes both calcium oxide and tricalcium phosphate. Calcium oxide is a basic oxide that can be employed to adjust pH or provide calcium ions in compounded formulations. Tricalcium phosphate, on the other hand, serves as a calcium and phosphate source and is commonly used as a filler or binder in oral dosage forms. By recognizing calcium oxide and tricalcium phosphate as members of the bulk compounding chemicals group, formulators can select appropriate compounds to achieve desired physicochemical properties and therapeutic outcomes in compounded medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2103
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate is classified as a calcium compound, reflecting an “isa” relationship that places calcium acetate within the broader category of calcium compounds. This categorization indicates that calcium acetate shares the chemical characteristics of calcium compounds and informs its use in medical applications, such as serving as a phosphate binder in patients with renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium compounds constitute a class of chemical substances that contain calcium, and this class includes calcium glycerophosphate, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide. Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium‑phosphate salt that can serve as a source of both minerals. Calcium acetate is commonly employed as a phosphate‑binding agent in patients with renal failure to reduce serum phosphate levels. Calcium oxide, also known as quicklime, can be used as a calcium supplement or antacid in various medical contexts. All three substances are categorized under the broader term “Calcium Compounds,” reflecting their shared elemental composition while each possesses distinct chemical properties and clinical applications.
